title: Zero Trust Architecture Lab: Engineering and Validation of a Dual-Site Security Homelab
summary: Engineering case study of a dual-site Zero Trust lab aligned with NIST SP 800-207, featuring layered access control, telemetry engineering, adversarial validation, and centralized monitoring using pfSense, Splunk, Traefik, Authelia, Suricata, and Cloudflare.
content:
# ZTA Lab - Engineering Case Study

**By Karim Abdel-Nasser Rady**

---

## Overview

This document describes the design, implementation, and operational engineering of a dual-site security lab built across two physical hosts and  Eight virtual machines, guided by **NIST SP 800-207**.

The project explores how access control is enforced across multiple independent layers, how telemetry is collected and normalized from heterogeneous sources, how log pipelines are designed for signal quality rather than volume, and how detection logic is structured to produce actionable visibility rather than alert noise.

The architecture operates on one core principle: no component is trusted based on network position alone. Every access request is authenticated, authorized, and logged regardless of where it originates.

---

## Design Goals

Before any component was deployed, a set of design questions shaped the architecture:

- How do you enforce identity verification at every access point without relying on network location as a trust signal?
- How do you build a log pipeline that delivers high-fidelity, enriched events without exhausting ingestion limits?
- How do you segment a multi-site environment so that a compromised host in one zone is isolated from management infrastructure in another?
- How do you validate that the monitoring pipeline captures events without introducing noise that obscures real signal?

The answers to these questions determined the component selection, network topology, log routing design, and detection logic described in this document.

---

## Threat Model and Scope

Defining the trust boundary explicitly is what separates engineered architecture from assembled tooling.

| Assumption | Detail |
|---|---|
| No implicit trust based on network position | A host inside the network receives no inherent authorization |
| Identity must be verified per session | TOTP required on every new access session |
| Contextual signals inform access decisions | Geographic origin and device posture are evaluated per request |
| Cloudflare control plane is assumed uncompromised | Edge enforcement is treated as a trusted policy boundary - see note below |
| Physical host compromise is out of scope | Hypervisor and Windows 11 host security are not modeled |
| East-west movement between zones is restricted by default | Cross-VLAN traffic requires explicit firewall rules |

> **Platform dependency note:** Cloudflare functions as the outermost enforcement layer and represents a significant point of trust concentration in this architecture. Compromise of the Cloudflare account, tunnel token, or Access configuration would materially undermine edge-layer controls. This threat is explicitly out of scope for this implementation and is acknowledged as an architectural dependency rather than a mitigated risk.

**Out of scope for this version:**

- Active Directory and Windows-based authentication chains
- Compromise of the Cloudflare account or tunnel token
- Supply chain attacks on Docker images or pfSense packages
- Full SOAR-based automated response

---

## Architecture

![High level.png](images/1778570934466-high-level.png)

### Physical Infrastructure

The lab spans two physical Windows 11 machines running VirtualBox, connected over a home network. Each machine hosts a pfSense 2.7 firewall with Suricata IDS and a set of VMs in isolated VLANs. An IKEv2 IPsec tunnel bridges the two sites, carrying log data and application traffic between them.

| Device | Role | RAM |
|---|---|---|
| Device 1 (D1) | Security gateway, SIEM host, access enforcement | 16 GB |
| Device 2 (D2) | User network, application servers, log aggregation | 16 GB |

### VM Inventory

| VM | Device | VLAN | IP | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| pfSense-D1 | D1 | Trunk | 192.168.10.1 / 192.168.20.1 | Firewall, IPsec, Suricata |
| siem-vm | D1 | 10 | 192.168.10.20 | Splunk SIEM, Vector D1 |
| services-vm | D1 | 20 | 192.168.20.10 | Traefik, Authelia, cloudflared |
| pfSense-D2 | D2 | Trunk | 192.168.30.1 / 192.168.40.1 | Firewall, IPsec, Suricata |
| appserver-vm | D2 | 30 | 192.168.30.50 | DVWA application server, auditd |
| user-vm-1 | D2 | 30 | 192.168.30.10 | Simulated user workstation |
| collector-vm | D2 | 30 | 192.168.30.100 | Vector D2 log aggregator |
| kali-vm | D2 | 40 | 192.168.40.10 | Isolated test client |

### Network Zones and Trust Model

| Zone | VLAN | Trust Level | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| MGMT | 10 | Restricted | Splunk SIEM and management infrastructure |
| SERVICES | 20 | Controlled | Access enforcement point (Traefik + Authelia) |
| USERS | 30 | Semi-trusted | User workstations, app server, log collector |
| ISOLATED | 40 | Untrusted | Isolated zone for traffic generation and testing |

The firewall policy between zones directly reflects the intended trust model:

| Source | Destination | Action | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| VLAN 10 | Any | Allow | Management has full network reach |
| VLAN 20 | VLAN 10 | Allow | Traefik routes to Splunk backend |
| VLAN 20 | Internet | Allow | cloudflared outbound tunnel |
| VLAN 30 | VLAN 10 | Allow | Log forwarding via HEC over IPsec |
| VLAN 30 | Internet | Allow | General application access |
| VLAN 40 | VLAN 10 | **Block** | Isolated zone restricted from SIEM |
| VLAN 40 | VLAN 20 | **Block** | Isolated zone restricted from access enforcement layer |
| VLAN 40 | VLAN 30 | Allow | Controlled access to user zone for testing |

> VLAN segmentation enforces zone boundaries but does not replace identity verification, MFA, or continuous monitoring - all of which are implemented as independent controls.

---

## Access Control Design

![Authentication & Access Flow.jpeg](images/1778570949277-authentication---access-flow.jpeg)

### Design Philosophy

The access control model applies three independent enforcement layers before any request reaches a backend. A failure at any layer terminates the request. The design goal is to minimize single-point access bypass through independent validation at each enforcement layer.

```
External Browser
  |
  v
[1] Cloudflare Edge
    - Context-aware access policy (identity + country + device posture)
    - Custom WAF rules (offensive tool User-Agent blocking)
    - Geographic restriction expression
    - Rate limiting scoped per hostname
  |
  v (policy pass only)
[2] cloudflared Tunnel
    - Outbound-only connection from services-vm to Cloudflare
    - No inbound firewall ports required on Device 1
    - Traffic proxied through Cloudflare network
  |
  v
[3] Traefik Reverse Proxy (services-vm)
    - forwardAuth middleware on every declared route
    - Delegates authentication to Authelia before proxying
    - File-based route provider for stability
  |
  v (forwardAuth pass only)
[4] Authelia MFA
    - TOTP required per session
    - Account lockout after 3 failed attempts within 2 minutes
    - 30-minute inactivity timeout - 8-hour maximum session
    - default_policy: deny - undeclared routes are blocked
  |
  v (MFA pass only)
[5] Backend Application
    - Splunk, DVWA, or Traefik dashboard
    - Accessible only via the above chain under this configuration
```

### Layer 1 - Cloudflare Edge

Cloudflare serves as the outermost policy enforcement point. All external access is routed through Cloudflare's network via a `cloudflared` tunnel. No direct inbound connections are accepted on Device 1, reducing the externally exposed attack surface to zero open ports.

Three independent checks are evaluated per request:

**Identity check** - An email-based allowlist is enforced through Cloudflare Access. Non-permitted identities are rejected at the edge before reaching the tunnel.

**Geographic check** - A custom WAF expression blocks requests originating outside the permitted country. This is an explicitly written expression for this deployment, not a default Cloudflare feature.

**Device posture check** - WARP client enrollment is required. Unenrolled devices are rejected regardless of credentials or geographic origin.

Additional WAF configuration:
- Custom rules block known offensive tool User-Agents (`sqlmap`, `nikto`, `hydra`, `gobuster`, `masscan`)
- Rate limiting blocks endpoints receiving more than 30 requests in 10 seconds, with a 1-minute ban
- All rules scoped to specific hostnames to avoid matching internal service traffic

This implements context-aware access enforcement combining identity, geographic origin, and device posture per request. It is not full enterprise ABAC - which typically incorporates dynamic risk scoring and role metadata - but applies multiple independent contextual signals to every request.

As noted in the threat model, Cloudflare represents a significant trust concentration point. Its effectiveness as an enforcement layer depends on the integrity of the account and Access configuration.

### Layer 2 - Traefik (Policy Enforcement Point)

Traefik v2.11 acts as the Policy Enforcement Point between the Cloudflare tunnel and backend applications. Every declared route uses `forwardAuth` middleware, which passes the request to Authelia for verification before proxying.

After Authelia approves a request, Traefik forwards verified identity headers (`Remote-User`, `Remote-Groups`, `Remote-Name`, `Remote-Email`) to the backend. Applications receive session identity without managing authentication themselves.

Route definitions use the file-based provider (`--providers.file.directory`) rather than the Docker socket provider, which proved unstable during deployment.

### Layer 3 - Authelia (Identity and Session Control)

Authelia enforces multi-factor authentication and manages session lifecycle. Configuration uses `default_policy: deny` - any route not explicitly declared in the access control rules is blocked by default.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| MFA method | TOTP (time-based one-time password) |
| Session expiration | 8 hours |
| Inactivity timeout | 30 minutes |
| Failed attempt lockout | 3 attempts within 2 minutes |
| Ban duration | 30 minutes |
| Remember me | Disabled |

### Legitimate User Access Lifecycle

A user connecting to a protected application from outside the network follows this flow:

1. Browser navigates to `splunk.<domain>` - Cloudflare evaluates identity, country, and device posture
2. On policy pass, the request is proxied through the cloudflared tunnel to Traefik on services-vm
3. Traefik evaluates the route - `forwardAuth` sends an authentication check to Authelia
4. If no valid session exists, Authelia redirects the user to the login portal at `auth.<domain>`
5. User enters credentials - Authelia verifies against the user database
6. User provides TOTP code - Authelia validates and creates a session
7. Authelia redirects back to the original URL with an authorization response header
8. Traefik receives the `forwardAuth` approval, attaches identity headers, and proxies the request to the Splunk backend at `192.168.10.20:8000`
9. Session persists for up to 8 hours with a 30-minute inactivity check

Under this configuration, the application backend on siem-vm is reachable only via the Traefik route with a valid Authelia session. Direct access to `192.168.10.20` from outside VLAN 10 is blocked at the firewall layer independently.

---

## Telemetry and Log Engineering

![Logs Flow Diagram.jpeg](images/1778570970941-logs-flow-diagram.jpeg)

### Log Sources

The monitoring pipeline ingests events from Seven distinct sources across both sites:

| Source | VM | Protocol | Port | Log Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linux syslog (auth, sudo) | user-vm-1, user-vm-2, appserver-vm | rsyslog UDP | 514 | System auth events |
| auditd (syscall events) | appserver-vm | rsyslog UDP | 514 | Command execution, file access |
| pfSense filterlog | pfSense-D1, pfSense-D2 | Syslog UDP | 515 | Firewall pass/block events |
| Suricata EVE JSON | pfSense-D1, pfSense-D2 | Syslog UDP | 516 | IDS alerts |
| Journald (D1 services) | siem-vm, services-vm | Vector journald | local | Service and container events |
| Docker container logs | services-vm | Vector Docker | local | Traefik, Authelia, cloudflared |
| Authelia audit log | services-vm | Vector file | local | Authentication decisions |
| Traefik access log | services-vm | Vector file | local | HTTP request logs |

### Pipeline Architecture

Log data flows through two Vector instances before reaching Splunk:

```
D2 Sources (VLAN 30)
  user-vm-1        --> rsyslog :514 --+
  user-vm-2        --> rsyslog :514 --+
  appserver-vm     --> rsyslog :514 --+--> collector-vm (Vector D2) --> Splunk HEC :8088
  pfSense-D2       --> syslog  :515 --+        (filter - parse - enrich)    (via IPsec)
  Suricata-D2      --> syslog  :516 --+

D1 Sources (VLAN 10/20)
  siem-vm          --> journald       --+
  services-vm      --> syslog  :514  --+
  pfSense-D1       --> syslog  :515  --+--> siem-vm (Vector D1) --> Splunk HEC :8088
  Suricata-D1      --> syslog  :516  --+        (filter - enrich)       (localhost)
  Authelia log     --> file           --+
  Traefik log      --> file           --+
```

The two-instance design is intentional. A single collector on D2 would require log traffic to traverse an untrusted path before encryption. Running Vector locally on each site and transmitting only over the IPsec tunnel keeps log delivery encrypted end-to-end and isolated from application traffic.


### Noise Reduction Strategy

Splunk Free limits ingestion to 500 MB/day. Without filtering, six VMs running normal system activity would saturate that limit before a single security-relevant event is collected. The first stage of every Vector pipeline drops high-volume, low-value events before they reach the enrichment layer.

| Category | Source | Reason Filtered |
|---|---|---|
| CRON job execution | All VMs | Scheduled tasks produce no security signal |
| apt / dpkg activity | All VMs | Package manager events are routine maintenance noise |
| NTP sync (systemd-timesyncd) | All VMs | Clock synchronization has no security value here |
| NetworkManager events | All VMs | Interface events are expected and non-actionable |
| snapd activity | All VMs | Background snap refresh produces no signal |

**Result: ~85% reduction in total log volume** while preserving all authentication, authorization, audit, firewall, and IDS events.

### Field Enrichment and Normalization

After filtering, VRL (Vector Remap Language) transforms parse raw syslog text into structured fields. This normalization step is what makes detection queries precise and maintainable - without it, every rule would require fragile regex against inconsistent raw text.

| Source | Enriched Fields Produced |
|---|---|
| SSH auth events | `auth_event`, `ssh.src_ip`, `ssh.user`, `ssh.port` |
| sudo commands | `sudo.user`, `sudo.run_as`, `sudo.cmd` |
| auditd syscalls | `audit_event` (`cmd_exec`, `net_connect`, `auth`) |
| Suricata EVE JSON | `alert_sig`, `alert_severity`, `alert_category` |
| pfSense filterlog | `firewall.src_ip`, `firewall.dst_ip`, `firewall.action`, `firewall.proto` |
| All events | `source_site`, `lab_zone` (for cross-site correlation) |

The `source_site` and `lab_zone` fields are particularly valuable in a multi-site environment - they allow a single SPL query to distinguish whether an event originated from VLAN 10 on D1 or VLAN 30 on D2 without requiring separate indexes or source filters.

---

## Detection Engineering Examples

The detections below are supported by the enriched telemetry pipeline described above. Each rule targets a specific behavioral pattern rather than a single log event. These are prototype rules written against structured fields - they have not been tuned against sustained production-volume data and should be treated accordingly.

---

**Detection 1 - SSH Brute Force**

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| Name | SSH brute force - threshold exceeded |
| Telemetry sources | rsyslog auth events from user-vm-1, user-vm-2, appserver-vm |
| Enriched fields | `auth_event=ssh_fail`, `ssh.src_ip`, `ssh.user`, `host` |
| Logic summary | Aggregate failed SSH authentication attempts per source IP per host over a 10-minute window. Fire when attempt count exceeds 5. Escalate to CRITICAL when count exceeds 20. |
| Signal / outcome | Identifies credential stuffing or automated brute force attempts against SSH-exposed hosts in VLAN 30 |

---

**Detection 2 - Brute Force Followed by Successful Authentication**

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| Name | Brute force to valid account - T1110 + T1078 |
| Telemetry sources | rsyslog auth events |
| Enriched fields | `auth_event` (ssh_fail / ssh_success), `ssh.src_ip`, `host` |
| Logic summary | For each source IP, collect both failure and success events within a sliding window. Flag any IP that transitions from repeated failures to a successful login on the same host. |
| Signal / outcome | Identifies successful authentication following a brute force sequence - a higher-confidence indicator than failed attempts alone |

---

**Detection 3 - Repeated Failed MFA Attempts**

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| Name | Repeated Authelia authentication failures |
| Telemetry sources | Authelia audit log via Vector file source on services-vm |
| Enriched fields | `auth_event=mfa_fail`, `authelia.username`, `authelia.remote_ip` |
| Logic summary | Count MFA failures per username and remote IP within a 5-minute window. Fire when failures exceed 3 from the same source, correlating with Authelia's internal lockout threshold. |
| Signal / outcome | Detects MFA enumeration or credential-stuffing attempts reaching the authentication portal despite passing Cloudflare edge checks |

---

**Detection 4 - Geographic Policy Violation Attempt**

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| Name | Access request from blocked geographic origin |
| Telemetry sources | Traefik access log and Cloudflare WAF logs forwarded via Vector |
| Enriched fields | `http.status_code=403`, `http.src_country`, `waf.rule_id` |
| Logic summary | Identify HTTP 403 responses attributed to the custom geographic WAF expression. Correlate request source country against the permitted country allowlist. |
| Signal / outcome | Provides visibility into blocked access attempts originating from outside the permitted region, useful for identifying reconnaissance activity or VPN bypass attempts |

---

**Detection 5 - Suricata IDS Alert Correlated with Firewall Deny**

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| Name | IDS alert with concurrent firewall block - coordinated access attempt |
| Telemetry sources | Suricata EVE JSON (pfSense-D2), pfSense filterlog (pfSense-D1 / D2) |
| Enriched fields | `alert_sig`, `alert_severity`, `firewall.src_ip`, `firewall.action=block`, `source_site`, `lab_zone` |
| Logic summary | Within a 5-minute window, correlate Suricata alerts and firewall block events sharing the same source IP across both sites. Require at least one IDS alert and one firewall deny from the same source. |
| Signal / outcome | Distinguishes active scanning or exploitation attempts from background noise by requiring confirmation across two independent telemetry sources |

---

**Detection 6 - Privileged Command Execution via Sudo**

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| Name | Sudo execution as root - privilege escalation indicator |
| Telemetry sources | rsyslog sudo events from appserver-vm, user-vm-1, user-vm-2 |
| Enriched fields | `auth_event=sudo`, `sudo.run_as=root`, `sudo.user`, `sudo.cmd`, `host` |
| Logic summary | Fire on any sudo execution where the target user is root. Enrich with the originating user and command for analyst review. Correlate with prior SSH authentication events from the same host to establish session context. |
| Signal / outcome | Identifies privilege escalation activity on Linux hosts, with command-level detail available from the `sudo.cmd` field |

---

![Screenshot (706).png](images/1778571095929-screenshot--706-.png)

## Validation

### Functional Validation

Functional validation confirmed that each control was active and correctly configured under normal operating conditions. These tests verify intended behavior, not adversarial resilience.

| Test | Expected Result | Observed |
|---|---|---|
| VLAN 40 to siem-vm (192.168.10.20) | Blocked by firewall | Connection timed out |
| VLAN 40 to services-vm (192.168.20.10) | Blocked by firewall | Connection timed out |
| External access without WARP enrollment | Rejected by Cloudflare | HTTP 403 |
| External access from outside permitted country | Blocked by WAF geo expression | HTTP 403 |
| Authelia login without valid TOTP | Session rejected | Login refused at MFA step |
| Route with no Authelia policy entry | Blocked by `default_policy: deny` | HTTP 403 from Traefik |
| IPsec tunnel log delivery | Splunk HEC receives D2 events | Events visible in Splunk with correct `source_site` field |

![Screenshot (701).png](images/1778571083138-screenshot--701-.png)

### Adversarial Simulation Scenarios

The following scenarios were run from kali-vm (VLAN 40) and from external test systems to confirm that controls behaved as designed under active traffic conditions. These are not penetration-test-grade assessments - they confirm functional control behavior against known test cases, not unknown attack vectors.

| Scenario | Source | Control Tested | Observed Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nmap SYN scan of VLAN 10 from VLAN 40 | kali-vm | pfSense inter-VLAN block rules | Scan returned all ports filtered - firewall deny events visible in Splunk |
| Hydra SSH brute force against appserver-vm | kali-vm | Splunk detection + rsyslog enrichment | SSH failure events aggregated in Splunk - brute force detection fired as expected |
| sqlmap scan via external domain | External host | Cloudflare WAF User-Agent rule | Request blocked at edge - HTTP 403 returned before reaching Traefik |
| Access attempt from restricted country via VPN | External host | Cloudflare geo WAF expression | Request blocked at edge - geo rule matched correctly |
| Repeated failed TOTP attempts | External host | Authelia lockout policy | Account locked after 3 failures within 2 minutes - ban applied for 30 minutes |
| Sudo escalation to root on appserver-vm (post-SSH access) | Simulated post-access | auditd + rsyslog sudo enrichment | `sudo.run_as=root` event appeared in Splunk with correct `sudo.cmd` field |

### Validation Limitations

These scenarios confirm that individually configured controls produced the expected responses under controlled test conditions. They do not establish:

- Resilience against unknown or novel attack techniques
- Correctness of controls under sustained high-volume load
- Coverage of all possible bypass paths or misconfigurations
- Behavior under partial system failure conditions

Detection rules are prototypes and have not been validated against production-volume data. Authelia and Traefik configuration correctness was validated functionally - formal security review of configuration files was not performed.

---

## NIST SP 800-207 Principles Applied

| Principle | Implementation |
|---|---|
| No implicit trust | All externally initiated requests pass through Cloudflare, Traefik, and Authelia regardless of network source |
| All communication secured | HTTPS via Traefik TLS - AES-256-GCM IPsec for inter-site traffic - outbound-only cloudflared tunnel |
| Access granted per session | TOTP required per session - 8-hour maximum - 30-minute inactivity timeout |
| Dynamic policy enforcement | Identity, geographic origin, and device posture evaluated per request at the Cloudflare layer |
| Continuous monitoring | Vector + Splunk provide real-time enriched event collection across all zones |
| Least privilege | Firewall rules default to deny between zones - access requires explicit policy |

---

## Operational Engineering Challenges

These are issues encountered during deployment, documented for their architectural or operational lessons rather than as step-by-step troubleshooting guides.

**Cloudflare WAF self-rate-limiting** - A rate limiting rule scoped to the root domain also matched Splunk's internal API calls (~50 requests per page load), causing the dashboard to trigger its own rate limit. Lesson: WAF rules must be scoped to specific application hostnames, not broad domain patterns.

**Vector startup race condition** - Vector initializing before Splunk's HEC endpoint was ready produced sustained healthcheck failures. Lesson: pipeline components with external dependencies require startup sequencing or health polling before initialization.

**rsyslog silent failure** - The rsyslog `imjournal` module fails silently when a required state directory is absent. The service reports as active while log forwarding is completely non-functional. Lesson: verify end-to-end log delivery at the SIEM, not just service status on the source host.

**Vector VRL syntax constraints** - Certain field access patterns and multi-line expressions valid in other pipeline tools are not supported in Vector 0.55. Lesson: validate VRL transforms in an isolated test pipeline before deploying against live log streams.

**Suricata EVE JSON forwarding reset** - Suricata loses its syslog forwarding configuration after each service restart or pfSense update. Lesson: syslog forwarding state for Suricata must be treated as volatile and verified after any pfSense maintenance event.

**Traefik Docker provider instability** - The Docker socket provider silently stopped serving routes after container restarts. Lesson: in this topology, file-based route configuration is more reliable than dynamic Docker provider discovery.

---

## Known Limitations

| Limitation | Impact |
|---|---|
| No EDR/XDR platform | Endpoint visibility relies on auditd syscall rules and rsyslog |
| Linux-only environment | No Windows attack surface - Active Directory, NTLM, and Kerberos are untested |
| No Active Directory | Authentication is local-account and SSH-key based only |
| No SOAR automation | Alert response is manual - detection fires but no automated containment |
| Splunk Free ingestion cap | 500 MB/day constrains log retention and high-volume scenarios |
| No hardware TAP or SPAN | Network visibility is IDS-based only - no full packet capture |
| Detection rules are prototypes | SPL rules have not been tuned against production-volume log data |
| Cloudflare trust concentration | Edge enforcement depends entirely on the integrity of the Cloudflare account and Access configuration |

---

## Key Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Physical machines | 2 |
| Virtual machines | 8 |
| VLANs / trust zones | 4 |
| Log sources integrated | 7 |
| Enriched fields produced by VRL | 15+ |
| Log volume reduction via noise filtering | ~85% |
| Access enforcement layers | 3 independent (Cloudflare - Traefik - Authelia) |
| Cross-site log delivery | Splunk HEC over IKEv2 IPsec tunnel |
| MITRE ATT&CK techniques with telemetry coverage | 14 |
| Detection rules implemented | 6 (prototype) |

---

## What's Next

**Near-term**
- Deploy Zeek for enriched network metadata visibility and east-west traffic analysis
- FIM integration across all VMs for file integrity monitoring
- Expand Linux endpoint telemetry with auditd, sudo, SSH, and process monitoring improvements
- Improve log correlation between Suricata alerts, firewall events, and authentication telemetry

**Medium-term**
- Deploy Keycloak alongside Authelia to introduce centralized identity management and role-based access control
- Add Zeek to Splunk parsing and enrichment pipelines for DNS, HTTP, TLS, and connection metadata
- Windows Server VM for Active Directory scenarios and Windows event log ingestion
- Expand SPL rule library with MITRE ATT&CK coverage heatmap
- Structured access policy documentation per application in Authelia
- Improve authentication visibility by correlating Cloudflare, Traefik, and Authelia session events

**Long-term**
- Replace Splunk Free with Elastic to remove the ingestion cap
- SOAR integration (n8n or Shuffle) for automated alert response
- Formal detection rule tuning against sustained log volume
- Introduce micro segmentation concepts for service-to-service communication control
- Add centralized asset inventory and vulnerability visibility for managed hosts
---

## Conclusions

This project demonstrates practical implementation across five engineering domains that are frequently treated as separate disciplines:

**Network architecture** - Designing a segmented multi-site environment where trust zones are enforced at the firewall layer rather than assumed from network position.

**Identity and access control** - Building a three-layer enforcement chain (Cloudflare context policy - Traefik forwardAuth - Authelia MFA) that applies consistently to every application route regardless of source, with independent validation at each layer.

**Telemetry engineering** - Designing a log pipeline that aggregates heterogeneous sources, normalizes them into a consistent field schema, filters noise systematically, and delivers enriched events to a central SIEM across an encrypted tunnel.

**Detection design** - Writing SPL rules against structured fields rather than raw text, targeting behavioral patterns across multiple event types rather than individual log lines.

**Operational troubleshooting** - Diagnosing infrastructure failures across a multi-component stack where failures are often silent and root causes require cross-layer investigation.

The core architectural observation is that the access model described here functions as intended only when all layers are correctly connected and operating together. Cloudflare alone enforces edge policy but has no visibility into internal routing. Authelia alone manages sessions but cannot enforce network segmentation. pfSense alone segments the network but cannot verify identity. Each control is necessary but insufficient on its own - the coherence of the system depends on the correct integration of all of them, and on the ongoing integrity of the platforms each layer depends upon.

---

*Stack: Splunk 9.3.2 - Vector 0.55.0 - Traefik v2.11 - Authelia - pfSense 2.7 - Suricata - Cloudflare Zero Trust - Docker - Ubuntu 22.04/24.04 - VirtualBox 7.x*

*ZTA Lab - Karim Abdel-Nasser Rady*
